transcription_cleaned:
Doctor: Good morning, I am Dr. Smith. Can you tell me what brings you in today?
Patient: Good morning, I have been having some abdominal pain for the past four days.
Doctor: I see. Can you describe the pain for me?
Patient: It is postprandial and sharp, and sometimes it radiates to my back.
Doctor: Okay. Can you tell me a little bit about your past medical history?
Patient: I have a past medical history of chronic pancreatitis, insulin-dependent diabetes, hypertension, peripheral arterial disease, and gout.
Doctor: I see. And what medications are you currently taking?
Patient: I am taking insulin, metformin 500 mg daily, lisinopril 40 mg daily, amlodipine 10 mg daily, metoprolol 25 mg twice a day, rosuvastatin 40 mg per day, aspirin 81 mg, clopidogrel 75 mg per day, and allopurinol 100 mg.
Doctor: Okay. And can you tell me about your family history?
Patient: My mother had insulin-dependent diabetes and hypertension, and my father had hypertension.
Doctor: Alright. Can you tell me about your alcohol and smoking habits?
Patient: I used to smoke a few cigarettes a day, but I quit over five years ago. I drink around four alcoholic beverages daily.
Doctor: Okay. And can you tell me about your previous visits to the emergency room?
Patient: Eighteen months ago, I went to the emergency room with postprandial abdominal pain.
Doctor: I see. And what was the outcome of that visit?
Patient: I had a CT scan of my abdomen and it showed necrotizing acute pancreatitis and a fluid collection between my pancreas and stomach. I was managed supportively and discharged.
Doctor: Alright. And did you have any other visits to the emergency room after that?
Patient: A few months later, I was admitted for diabetic ketoacidosis secondary to pancreatic necrosis.
Doctor: Okay. And did you have any abdominal imaging done at that visit?
Patient: No, I did not.
Doctor: Alright. And did you have any follow-up CT scans after your initial episode of acute pancreatitis?
Patient: Yes. I had a repeat CT scan five months after my initial episode, and it showed no change in size of the pancreatic pseudocyst.
Doctor: Okay. And can you describe the pain you are experiencing now compared to the pain you experienced eighteen months ago?
Patient: The pain is of different character than the original episode of acute pancreatitis, but I am unable to specifically describe how.
Doctor: Alright. I am going to do a physical examination now.
Patient: Yes, that is fine.
Doctor: Okay, I have finished the examination. You are alert but tachycardic at 110 beats per minute and have decreased breath sounds at the bilateral lung bases. Your abdomen is nontender but mildly distended. Your neurologic examination is unremarkable.
Patient: Okay.
Doctor: Based on your symptoms and medical history, I am going to order a CT scan of your abdomen to evaluate further.
Patient: Okay.
Doctor: In the meantime, I want you to rest and avoid eating solid foods. I will also adjust your medications to help manage your pain.
Doctor: I will see you again after the results of the CT scan are back. Is there anything else you would like to ask me?
Patient: No, that is all. Thank you.
Doctor: You are welcome. Take care.
clinical_summary: 53-year-old male with chronic pancreatitis, insulin-dependent diabetes, hypertension, peripheral arterial disease, and gout presented with four days of postprandial sharp LUQ abdominal pain radiating to back. Prior history of necrotizing pancreatitis with pseudocyst and DKA from pancreatic necrosis. CT abdomen revealed enlargement of pancreatic pseudocyst to 15.9 x 10.4 cm compressing splenic vasculature. After endoscopic cystogastrostomy, he developed respiratory distress, exudative pleural effusion, and a subcapsular splenic hematoma with abscess requiring exploratory laparotomy and splenectomy.

differential_diagnosis.rationale: CT abdomen demonstrated growth of the known pseudocyst from 7.1 cm to 15.9 cm with mass effect on the stomach and splenic vasculature, directly explaining the acute postprandial abdominal pain.

Elevated lipase of 145 U/L and history of chronic pancreatitis with ongoing alcohol use make recurrent acute pancreatitis a strong consideration, though imaging favored pseudocyst enlargement as the primary etiology.

Post-cystogastrostomy development of fever, leukocytosis, and CT findings of a subcapsular splenic hematoma communicating with a subdiaphragmatic collection indicated secondary splenic infection, confirmed intraoperatively as a 300 mL splenic abscess.

drug_interactions.recommendation: Patients receiving an insulin secretagogue or insulin may require lower doses of the insulin secretagogue or insulin.

final_report.subjective: 53-year-old African American male with chronic pancreatitis, insulin-dependent diabetes, hypertension, peripheral arterial disease, and gout presented with four days of postprandial sharp left upper quadrant abdominal pain radiating to his back. He described the pain as different in character from his prior episode of necrotizing acute pancreatitis 18 months ago. He had a known pancreatic pseudocyst that was stable on prior imaging. Home medications included insulin, metformin, lisinopril, amlodipine, metoprolol, rosuvastatin, aspirin, clopidogrel, and allopurinol. Social history notable for former smoking (quit >5 years) and approximately four alcoholic beverages daily. Family history of diabetes and hypertension in both parents.
final_report.objective: Vitals: T 37.4C, BP 161/88, HR 90, O2 sat 100% on room air. Exam: alert, decreased bilateral basilar breath sounds, abdomen nontender but mildly distended, neurologic exam unremarkable. Labs: WBC 14.7, Hgb 16.7, glucose 221, HbA1c 11.3%, bicarbonate 23 (no DKA), lipase 145 U/L, electrolytes/renal/liver function unremarkable. CT abdomen: pseudocyst enlarged from 7.1 cm to 15.9 x 10.4 cm displacing stomach and compressing splenic vasculature. Post-cystogastrostomy: respiratory distress with O2 sat 93% on 15L NRB, WBC rose to 19.8, new left pleural effusion on CXR. Thoracentesis: exudative (LDH 591, amylase 115, pH 7.58). Follow-up CT: decreased pseudocyst with appropriate stent, new large left subdiaphragmatic collection communicating with subcapsular splenic hematoma. Operative findings: 300 mL splenic abscess, grade IV splenic laceration, significant peripancreatic inflammation.
final_report.assessment: Acute abdominal pain from enlarging pancreatic pseudocyst in the setting of chronic pancreatitis with ongoing alcohol use. Post-cystogastrostomy complication of splenic abscess with subcapsular hematoma, likely from peripancreatic inflammatory erosion into the spleen given its proximity to the pseudocyst. Exudative pleural effusion secondary to sympathetic response from pancreatitis. Poorly controlled diabetes (HbA1c 11.3%) exacerbated by pancreatic insufficiency.
final_report.plan: Endoscopic cystogastrostomy performed with stent placement. Exploratory laparotomy and splenectomy for septic splenic hematoma. Pleural effusion managed with tube thoracostomy. Prophylactic antibiotics with levofloxacin and metronidazole. Home medications continued except clopidogrel held during admission. Pain managed with acetaminophen, oxycodone, and IV morphine PRN. Post-splenectomy vaccinations. Discharge after protracted postoperative recovery with outpatient follow-up for surgical complications and medication management. Counsel regarding alcohol cessation given role in chronic pancreatitis.
